text: Agile is a philosophy of software development based on four values from the Agile Manifesto: individuals and interactions over processes and tools, working software over comprehensive documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, responding to change over following a plan. Agile does not mean no process — it means lightweight processes that serve people, not the reverse.

Scrum is an agile framework using fixed-length iterations called Sprints (1-4 weeks). Three roles: Product Owner (owns the backlog, defines priorities), Scrum Master (facilitates, removes impediments, not a manager), Development Team (self-organizing, cross-functional). Five events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, Sprint itself.

Scrum — Product Backlog: an ordered list of everything needed in the product. Items at the top are more detailed and ready to work on. Items at the bottom are vague and will be refined later. The Product Owner is solely responsible for ordering the backlog. Backlog refinement is continuous — not a one-time event.

Scrum — Sprint Planning: the team selects backlog items they forecast they can complete in the Sprint and creates a Sprint Goal — a single objective that gives the Sprint coherence. The Sprint Goal is fixed; the specific items achieving it can be negotiated. A Sprint without a clear goal produces a collection of unrelated tasks with no direction.

Scrum — Definition of Done (DoD): a shared understanding of what complete means. Without DoD, done means different things to different people — code complete, tested, reviewed, deployed, documented. DoD prevents undone work from accumulating invisibly. If an item does not meet DoD it does not ship — partial work that ships creates more problems than it solves.

Scrum — Sprint Retrospective: the team inspects its own process and identifies one or two concrete improvements for the next Sprint. Not a complaint session — a structured improvement cycle. The most effective retrospectives are specific: not 'we need better communication' but 'we will add a 15-minute sync when a PR has been open more than 24 hours'.

Kanban is an agile method focused on visualizing workflow and limiting work in progress (WIP). Unlike Scrum, Kanban has no fixed iterations or roles. Core practices: visualize all work on a board, limit WIP per stage to expose bottlenecks, manage flow by measuring cycle time. Kanban suits operations and support teams with unpredictable incoming work better than Scrum.

Agile anti-patterns: Dark Scrum — going through Scrum motions without the values, using ceremonies as control mechanisms rather than collaboration tools. Mini-Waterfall — planning the entire sprint upfront, no adaptation. Velocity as a performance metric — velocity measures team capacity for planning, not team performance or productivity. Feature factory — shipping features continuously without measuring outcomes or learning from users.
